content:
It sounds like your incoming mail (IMAP/POP) settings are fine since the inbox is syncing, but the problem is likely with your outgoing mail (SMTP) configuration. Most email apps require you to set the SMTP server, port, and authentication separately from the inbox settings.

Here are a few things to check:

SMTP Server Details – Make sure the server address, port (often 465 or 587), and encryption type (SSL/TLS) match your email provider’s requirements.

Authentication – Many providers require “Use same username and password as incoming mail” to be enabled for sending.

Firewall/Antivirus – Occasionally, security software can block outgoing connections. Temporarily disabling it (for testing) can help identify if that’s the cause.

Provider Restrictions – Some providers block SMTP on mobile or public networks unless you use their official app or enable special settings like “Allow less secure apps.”

Once your SMTP settings are correct, sending should work right away. I had a similar issue after migrating my emails — fixed it by adjusting the port and enabling authentication. And while I was troubleshooting, I had a Kuromi wallpaper on my desktop, which at least kept the process a bit more cheerful!

content:
If you can receive emails in Thunderbird but can’t send them from your Spectrum account, the issue is almost always tied to the SMTP configuration. Spectrum’s outgoing mail settings are quite specific, so even a small mismatch can prevent sending.

Here’s what to double-check:

SMTP Server – mail.twc.com or the server address provided by Spectrum for your region.

Port – Use 587 with STARTTLS (or TLS) encryption.

Authentication – Make sure “Use same username and password as incoming” is enabled.

Username Format – Sometimes Spectrum requires the full email address as the username (e.g., you@example.com).

ISP Restrictions – If you’re on a different internet provider’s network, Spectrum may block outgoing mail unless you’re using authentication with the correct port and encryption.

I remember reading a troubleshooting article on Universal Magzines online about Spectrum’s email setup in Thunderbird, and it highlighted that re-entering credentials in the SMTP section (not just the incoming section) often resolves this issue.

Once the SMTP settings are updated correctly, sending should work without any further tweaks.

content:
Wayne Mery said
Rui, are you still seeing this problem? And why do/did you think this is a fingerprint issue?

Hey there. Sorry, I completely forgot about it.

Not, I'm past this issue. Let me explain. I use Obtainium package manager on Android. It connects to github/gitlab and it allows me to install packages without having to use a playstore, straight from the source. But thunderbird's github instance also provides K9-Mail's package. It just so happens that Obtainium was trying to update my TB's instance with K9's updated package available on github. I just set Obtainium to filter by name and now it only fetches a new package if package's name starts with thunderbird. All good now. Not a Mozilla issue. Thanks a bunch. Cheers

Peter said
I must either type the full recipient address or manually select a contact; unlike other email clients, it does not suggest addresses from previous correspondence.

1. The following steps light be helpful: "Settings" → "Composition" → "Addressing" section → "When addressing messages, look for matching entries in:" → Tick "Local Address Books" to enable autocomplete. → Tick "Automatically add outgoing email addresses to my: ___". to make Thunderbird provide suggestion from previous correspondence.

Please see the attached image.

2. The following link might be helpful: Addressing an Email https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/addressing-email

Please see "Adding recipients from your address book" section in the above link. Quotation from the above section: "Type search words in the address field of your choice, and recipient autocomplete will assist you."

content: I believe the issue is that every email address must be saved in your personal address book. Otherwise, you have to type or copy-and-paste addresses each time, which is awkward and frustrating. The Windows version automatically remembers addresses you’ve used before and suggests them for autofill, but the Android app doesn’t. To fix this, the same autofill feature needs to be added to Android.
